toolUseResult.content:
Claims System · Visual Reference

The whole system on one page — domain model, coverage rules as data, the gates-then-math adjudication pipeline, the cost-share switch, a claim worked end to end, the accumulator's memory, the decisions that shaped the engine, the state machines, and the 27-cycle TDD build order. Every section is example-induced with real integer-cents numbers. Use it for the round-2 pairing walkthrough and to align any future agent (or human) on the model.

01 · The domain model

Eight entities, cleanly split by what they are: rules are static config, facts are what was submitted, the accumulator is running memory, and adjudications are append-only decisions. That separation is what makes adjudication deterministic and replayable.

EntityLayerOne-line responsibility
MemberidentityThe insured person who submits claims; holds PHI (name, dob).
PolicyrulesBinds a member to coverage for one plan year — active dates, deductible, OOP max.
CoverageRulerulesHow one service is covered: cost-share mechanism, deductible applicability, unit-typed limit, prior-auth.
ClaimfactsA submission envelope grouping line items; status derived, fingerprinted.
LineItemfactsOne service on a claim — the actual unit of adjudication.
AccumulatormemoryPer-member-per-plan-year running totals: deductible met, OOP met, per-service limit used.
AdjudicationdecisionsThe immutable decision for a line item (status, payable, member owes, reason, explanation). Append-only.
DisputedecisionsA member challenge that reopens a line item and preserves the prior decision.

What this proves

  • Four layers, four responsibilities. Rules (static) · facts (claim/line) · memory (accumulator) · decisions (adjudication, append-only). A decision is always reproducible from rules + facts + a snapshot of memory.
  • The decision lives on the line item, not the claim. LineItem 1—N Adjudication is what lets a re-adjudication append a new row while the original is preserved — claim status is derived, never the source of truth.
  • One policy per member per plan year (Member 1—1 Policy), and one accumulator set per plan year (Member 1—N Accumulator) — limits exhaust correctly across claims within the year.

02 · Coverage rules are data, not code

Coverage is a typed config record — not hardcoded branches, not a DSL. A discriminated cost_share union forces exactly one mechanism per service, and a unit-typed limit expresses both dollar caps and visit caps without nullable junk fields. 

type CoverageRule = {
  policy_id: string;
  service_code: string;          // closed 12-entry catalog; unlisted → NO_COVERAGE
  covered: boolean;
  excluded: boolean;             // explicit EXCLUDED beats "not covered"
  cost_share:                    // discriminated union — one mechanism per service
    | { type: "full_coverage" }
    | { type: "copay"; copay_cents: number }
    | { type: "coinsurance"; rate: number };   // member share, 0.0–1.0
  applies_deductible: boolean;   // copay → usually false; coinsurance → usually true
  limit:                         // unit-typed — the load-bearing addition
    | { unit: "none" }
    | { unit: "dollars"; amount_cents: number } // "$Y/yr" — the brief's example
    | { unit: "visits"; count: number };        // "20 PT visits/yr"
  requires_prior_auth: boolean;
};
ServiceCost-shareDeductibleLimitPrior auth
Annual physical / preventivefull coverageno—no
Primary care visit$25 copayno—no
Specialist visit$50 copayno—no
Urgent care$50 copayno—no
Emergency room$300 copayno—no
Lab / X-ray20% coinsuranceyes—no
MRI / advanced imaging20% coinsuranceyes—yes
Outpatient surgery20% coinsuranceyes—yes
Inpatient hospital20% coinsuranceyes—yes
Physical therapy$40 copayno20 visits/yrno
Chiropractic$25 copayno$1,500/yrno
Adult dentalexcluded———
Mini-example — the brief's "$Y/year"

Chiropractic's $1,500/yr cap is just one row's limit field:

{ service_code: "CHIROPRACTIC",
  cost_share: { type: "copay", copay_cents: 2500 },
  limit: { unit: "dollars", amount_cents: 150000 } }

What this proves

  • The discriminated union encodes a real-world fact: a benefit uses exactly one cost-share mechanism. Nullable copay_cents + coinsurance_rate fields would make the illegal "both set / neither set" states representable; the union makes them unrepresentable.
  • The unit on limit is load-bearing. A dollars-only field cannot express "20 PT visits/year" — the most common real-world cap. One discriminator covers both, and the adjudicator picks the column from it.
  • 12 rules exercise every branch: full_coverage, copay, coinsurance, visit-limit, dollar-limit, prior-auth, and excluded — plus any unlisted code falling through to NO_COVERAGE. Adding a 13th service is a data row, not a code change.

03 · The adjudication pipeline — gates, then math

Each line item walks one ordered pipeline. The first half is gates — any one fails, the line short-circuits to a clean denial. The second half is math — it always runs to completion and moves money.

#StepConditionOn fail → reason codeKind
0Duplicatefingerprint not already adjudicatedDUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM, payable 0GATE
1Policy activeeffective ≤ serviceDate ≤ terminationPOLICY_NOT_ACTIVE, payable 0GATE
2Rule existsrule found for service_codeNO_COVERAGE, payable 0GATE
3Covered / not excluded!excluded && coveredEXCLUDED / NO_COVERAGEGATE
4Prior auth!requires_prior_auth || prior_auth_presentPRIOR_AUTH_REQUIRED, payable 0GATE
5Limit remainingvisits: used < count · dollars: used < amountLIMIT_EXCEEDED, payable 0GATE
6Compute allowedallowed = billed (v1)—MATH
7Cost-share switchfull_coverage | copay | coinsurance—MATH
7bDollar-limit straddledollars + plan-pay exceeds remainingcap plan, shortfall→member, line stays APPROVEDMATH
8OOP capmember share would pass oop_maxrefund excess to plan, OOP_MAX_REACHEDMATH
9Build deltasdeductible / oop / limit increments—MATH
Mini-example — short-circuit at the prior-auth gate
An MRI line, billed_cents: 120000, but prior_auth_present: false. Step 4 fails. The line stops immediately — steps 5–9 never run, no math, no deductible touched. Result: DENIED, PRIOR_AUTH_REQUIRED, payable 0, member owes 0, deltas all 0.

What this proves

  • The pipeline has two halves with different jobs: gates 0–5 can only deny and stop; math 6–9 can only compute and continue. No step does both.
  • A failed gate is a clean decision (HTTP 200 + reason), not an error. It short-circuits before any money moves — so a denied line is cheap and fully explainable.
  • The straddle (7b) and OOP cap (8) are the only steps that adjust rather than deny: they cap a payable and re-attribute the shortfall, leaving the line APPROVED.

04 · The cost-share switch

Step 7 is one switch on cost_share.type — exactly one mechanism per rule, never stacked. The deductible is touched by coinsurance only; copay waives it and full_coverage ignores it.

typeDeductibleMember owesPlan paysReasons
full_coverageuntouched0allowed[APPROVED]
copaywaived; accrues to OOP onlymin(copay, allowed)allowed − member[APPROVED, COPAY_APPLIED]
coinsuranceapplied first (if applies_deductible)dedPortion + coinsPortionallowed − member[APPROVED, DEDUCTIBLE_APPLIED?, COINSURANCE_APPLIED]
The coinsurance formula — the only non-trivial branch
remainingDeductible = max(0, deductible_cents − deductible_met_cents)
dedPortion   = applies_deductible ? min(remainingDeductible, allowed) : 0
remainder    = allowed − dedPortion
coinsPortion = Math.round(rate * remainder)        // round half-up
member       = dedPortion + coinsPortion
plan         = allowed − member                    // computed last → shares always sum
(a) full_coverage — $200 preventive line
allowed = 20000c. Member 0c, plan 20000c. Deductible and OOP untouched by the mechanism. [APPROVED].
(b) copay — $25 copay on a $180 visit
allowed = 18000c, copay 2500c. Member = min(2500, 18000) = 2500c; plan = 15500c. Deductible waived; 2500c accrues to OOP. [APPROVED, COPAY_APPLIED].
(c) coinsurance — 20% on a $1200 MRI, $300 deductible remaining
allowed = 120000c, remainingDeductible = 30000c. dedPortion = 30000c; remainder = 90000c; coinsPortion = round(0.20 × 90000) = 18000c; member = 48000c; plan = 120000 − 48000 = 72000c. [APPROVED, DEDUCTIBLE_APPLIED, COINSURANCE_APPLIED].

What this proves

  • One rule = one mechanism. The switch is exhaustive over a discriminated union — incoherent "copay + coinsurance" rules can't be expressed, let alone mis-adjudicated.
  • plan = allowed − member is computed last, so member + plan always equal the allowed amount — no lost or duplicated cent, even with odd-cents rounding.
  • Only coinsurance reads/draws the deductible. Copay deliberately waives it and routes the flat charge to OOP — matching how real office-visit copays behave.

05 · A claim, end to end

The flagship worked example: one member, three line items, accumulators moving line by line. Watch the deductible fill and the money split — then the three lines roll up to one claim status.

Policy
Deductible $500 (50000c) · OOP max $3000 (300000c)
Starting accumulators
DEDUCTIBLE met 20000c · OOP met 20000c · no limit rows
L1 · unknown code — no matching rule
Gate 2 fails → NO_COVERAGE. DENIED, payable 0c, member 0c. Deltas all 0.
Accumulators: DEDUCTIBLE 20000c → 20000c · OOP 20000c → 20000c (unchanged)
L2 · PCP_VISIT — $25 copay on $180 (18000c)
copay = min(2500, 18000) = member 2500c; plan 15500c. Deductible waived; copay → OOP. [APPROVED, COPAY_APPLIED]
Accumulators: DEDUCTIBLE 20000c → 20000c · OOP 20000c → 22500c (+2500)
L3 · MRI — 20% coinsurance on $1200 (120000c), prior_auth_present=true
remainingDeductible = 50000 − 20000 = 30000c; dedPortion = 30000c; remainder = 90000c; coinsPortion = round(0.20 × 90000) = 18000c; member 48000c; plan 72000c. OOP check: 22500 + 48000 = 70500c < 300000 → no clamp. [APPROVED, DEDUCTIBLE_APPLIED, COINSURANCE_APPLIED]
Accumulators: DEDUCTIBLE 20000c → 50000c (+30000, met) · OOP 22500c → 70500c (+48000)
LineStatusPayableMember owes
L1DENIED$0$0
L2APPROVED$155$25
L3APPROVED$720$480
ClaimPARTIALLY_APPROVED$875$505

What this proves

  • Cross-line state is real: L3 sees the deductible L2 left alone, draws the full remaining 30000c, and pushes it to met. Lines are processed in order against a single working snapshot.
  • Every covered line satisfies the invariant payable + member == billed ($155+$25=$180; $720+$480=$1200). The denied line is 0/0.
  • A mix of DENIED + APPROVED rolls up to PARTIALLY_APPROVED — plan pays $875, member owes $505. Re-running the same claim on the same starting snapshot reproduces these exact numbers.

06 · The accumulator — the system's memory

One table keyed by member_id + plan_year, with a dimension discriminator per row. This is what makes limits exhaust correctly across separate claims within a year, and it stores dollar limits and visit limits in different columns of the same shape.

dimensiontrackscolumn used
DEDUCTIBLEdeductible metused_cents
OOPout-of-pocket metused_cents
LIMIT:<service_code>per-service usageused_cents (dollar limit) or used_count (visit limit)

Row shape: (member_id, plan_year, dimension, used_cents, used_count). The rule's limit.unit picks the column — dollars → used_cents += plan pay; visits → used_count += 1. LIMIT: rows are created lazily on first use.

Worked example — flagship claim (policy: deductible $500 / 50000c, OOP max $3000 / 300000c)
BEFORE
DEDUCTIBLEused_cents 20000
OOPused_cents 20000
(no LIMIT rows yet)
→
AFTER (PCP copay + MRI coinsurance lines)
DEDUCTIBLEused_cents 50000
OOPused_cents 70500

L2 — PCP_VISIT, $25 copay: copay waives the deductible and accrues to OOP only → OOP += 2500 (20000 → 22500). Deductible untouched.
L3 — MRI, 20% coinsurance, applies_deductible: remaining deductible 30000c drawn first → DEDUCTIBLE += 30000 (20000 → 50000, now met); coinsurance 18000c on the remainder → member 48000c → OOP += 48000 (22500 → 70500).

Dollar vs visit columns: had a Chiropractic line landed, it would write LIMIT:CHIROPRACTIC.used_cents += plan pay. A PT line writes LIMIT:PHYSICAL_THERAPY.used_count += 1 — same row shape, the unit decides which counter moves.

What this proves

  • The dimension discriminator avoids a column-per-service-code schema. One row shape handles deductible, OOP, and N per-service limits; a new limited service adds a lazily-created LIMIT: row, not a migration.
  • Cross-claim memory is the whole point. The before-state isn't theoretical — it's a prior claim's residue. Limits exhaust correctly because every claim reads then increments the same keyed totals.
  • One shape, two units. used_cents vs used_count keeps dollar caps and visit caps in the same table while preserving the unit's meaning — no float, no overloaded single column.

07 · Decisions that shaped the engine

Three resolved judgment calls behind the engine's behavior — each one trades a real alternative for determinism and explainability under a 48-hour, no-reviewer-queue scope.

Decision #8
Prior-auth missing = clean DENY, not NEEDS_REVIEW
A required-auth line with prior_auth_present: false becomes a clean DENIED carrying PRIOR_AUTH_REQUIRED, payable 0.
Why: there is no human-reviewer queue in scope. A NEEDS_REVIEW line would never resolve and would freeze claim aggregation at UNDER_REVIEW forever. A clean deny is deterministic and fully explainable.
Decision #12
Adjudication outcomes are DECISIONS (HTTP 200), not errors
Every denial returns 200 with status + reason + explanation. Only malformed/identity-failed input is 4xx.
InputHTTPOutcome
prior_auth_present: false200denied line, PRIOR_AUTH_REQUIRED + explanation
prior_auth_present omitted200adjudicated as auth present (default true)
billed_cents: "abc"400intake error, nothing persisted
Why: an HTTP error explains nothing to a member and would break "5 line items, 1 denied" and "explain why it was denied." A denied line is a processed line.
Decision #13
prior_auth_present defaults to true (absence = auth present)
When a line omits prior_auth_present, treat it as true. The PRIOR_AUTH_REQUIRED path fires only on an explicit false.
Why: most services don't require prior auth, so a true default keeps the common input frictionless; callers assert false only when relevant. (Documented as a demo simplification — real systems treat absence as unauthorized.)

What this proves

  • Every decision is grounded in a scope constraint (no reviewer queue, 48h budget), not preference — and each is reversible at a single seam if the constraint lifts.
  • The decision/error boundary is the engine's core contract: business denials are data (200 + reason), only bad input is a protocol error (4xx).
  • The true default makes the happy path frictionless while keeping the denial path one explicit false away — the divergence from real systems is named, not hidden.

08 · Immutability, disputes & PHI

Decisions are append-only, each line's decision-plus-accumulator-write is one transaction, and PHI is kept structurally unreachable by the engine. Together these make the system auditable, consistent, and honest about sensitive data without over-building.

(a) Adjudications are append-only

Re-adjudication never mutates the original row. It reverses the line's original accumulator deltas, reapplies the new ones, and appends a new Adjudication. History is the audit trail.

(b) One transaction per line item

The decision write and every accumulator increment (deductible / OOP / limit) commit together in a single better-sqlite3 transaction. Synchronous single-writer → no interleaving, no half-applied decision.

(c) PHI is structurally unreachable

Member.name/dob and Claim.provider/diagnosis_code are sensitive. The engine input type keys on member_id only — it cannot read PHI because those fields aren't in its input shape. Column-level encryption-at-rest is documented as a candidate, not over-built for a 24–48h take-home.

Mini-example — disputing a denied MRI line

The original DENIED row is preserved verbatim; the new APPROVED row is appended with a DISPUTED_OVERRIDE reason, and the line's accumulator deltas are reversed then reapplied — both writes in one transaction.

What this proves

  • Append-only is the audit story. You can always answer "what did we decide, when, and why" — the original decision survives every dispute, so there's no destructive overwrite to reconstruct.
  • Atomicity is per line item. Decision + accumulator increments are one transaction, so the accumulator can never drift from the decisions that produced it — even mid-claim.
  • PHI minimization is enforced by the type system, not by discipline. The engine literally has no field to read diagnosis_code from; encryption-at-rest is named as the next step rather than half-implemented.

09 · State machines & aggregation

The engine produces the line-item state directly; the claim state is derived by aggregating its lines — never set directly. NEEDS_REVIEW is now reachable only through a dispute reopen.

Line-item lifecycle
Line outcomesDerived claim status
every line DENIEDDENIED
every line APPROVED (full payable)APPROVED
mix of APPROVED + DENIED, or any dollar-straddle partialPARTIALLY_APPROVED
any line NEEDS_REVIEW / disputed re-adjudicatingUNDER_REVIEW
every payable line PAID, none openPAID
Mini-example: the worked claim from §05 — lines [DENIED, APPROVED, APPROVED] → a mix → claim PARTIALLY_APPROVED. Flip L2/L3 to disputed and re-adjudicating → claim UNDER_REVIEW.

What this proves

  • Claim state is derived, not stored: claimStatusFrom(lines) is a pure aggregation, killing the "claim says approved but lines say denied" class of bug.
  • PARTIALLY_APPROVED is claim-level only — never a line state. A straddled line is itself APPROVED with a LIMIT_EXCEEDED note.
  • NEEDS_REVIEW survives as a state but is now reached only via a dispute reopen — prior-auth-missing is a clean DENIED (decision #8), so it never freezes a fresh claim.

10 · The build order — 27 test-first cycles

The engine is test-driven one behavior per red→green cycle, in this order. Cycles 1–25 are pure (a plain object, no DB); only 26–27 touch SQLite. This is the coding checklist.

Gates · cycles 1–7 — pure denials
  1. full_coverage line → member 0, plan = billed
  2. no rule → NO_COVERAGE, payable 0, DENIED
  3. excluded rule → EXCLUDED, payable 0
  4. covered == false → NO_COVERAGE, payable 0
  5. service date outside policy window → POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE
  6. requires_prior_auth && prior_auth_present == false → PRIOR_AUTH_REQUIRED, clean DENY
  7. duplicate fingerprint → DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM, payable 0, original untouched
Cost-share · cycles 8–13 — single line, fresh accumulators
  1. copay, allowed > copay → member = copay, plan = allowed − copay, deductible untouched
  2. copay, allowed < copay → member = allowed, plan = 0 (min clamp)
  3. coinsurance, deductible already met → member = round(rate·allowed), plan = rest
  4. coinsurance, allowed < remaining deductible → all to deductible, coins 0, plan 0
  5. coinsurance deductible crossing → split ded + coins; shares sum to allowed
  6. coinsurance odd cents (20% of 3333) → deterministic rounding; member + plan == allowed
Limits · cycles 14–17
  1. visits, used < count → approved, limit_inc = 1
  2. visits, used ≥ count → LIMIT_EXCEEDED, payable 0 (whole-visit, no straddle)
  3. dollars, payable within remaining → approved, limit_inc = plan pay
  4. dollars straddle → plan caps at remaining, shortfall→member, LIMIT_EXCEEDED, line APPROVED
OOP cap · cycles 18–19
  1. member share would pass oop_max → excess refunded to plan, OOP_MAX_REACHED
  2. accumulator already at oop_max → next line plan pays 100%, member 0
Cross-line / determinism · cycles 20–21
  1. two coinsurance lines, one claim → line 2 sees line 1's advanced deductible
  2. re-run identical claim on identical snapshot → identical results
Aggregation · cycles 22–25 — line[] → claim
  1. all approved → claim APPROVED
  2. all denied → claim DENIED
  3. mix approved + denied → claim PARTIALLY_APPROVED
  4. straddled-partial + full approved → claim PARTIALLY_APPROVED
Writeback / integration · cycles 26–27 — SQLite
  1. full claim through the real store: accumulators persist, adjudications append-only, one txn
  2. dispute reopens a line, re-adjudicates, preserves the original immutably (last)

What this proves

  • The order is gates → cost-share → limits → OOP → cross-line → aggregation → persistence: each cycle adds exactly one behavior on top of green, so a failing test always names the behavior that broke.
  • Cycles 1–25 need no database — they run against a plain accumulator snapshot object. The pure core is fully proven before SQLite ever appears.
  • Determinism (cycle 21) and immutable disputes (cycle 27) are explicit tests, not hopes: re-running a claim on the same snapshot must reproduce identical cents.

+

02 · Coverage rules are data, not code
- Two bounded contexts. The adjudication context is pure
- functions, no I/O. The submission context handles the wire
-    envelope. The seam between them is the only place persistence happens.
-    This separation is what keeps the engine testable.

-
  • CoverageRule is data, not code. A typed config object — not a DSL — is the 80/20 within 48h.

-
  • Accumulator is the system's short-term memory. Reading it before adjudication and writing it after is the only transactional step the engine has.

-
  • The Claim → LineItem 1:N relationship is why partial approvals exist. The system grades each line independently and rolls up.

-
  • Dispute doesn't mutate the original adjudication — it reopens a line item and produces a new one with a DISPUTED_OVERRIDE reason. History is preserved.

+
  • The discriminated union encodes a real-world fact: a benefit uses exactly one cost-share mechanism. Nullable copay_cents + coinsurance_rate fields would make the illegal "both set / neither set" states representable; the union makes them unrepresentable.

+
  • The unit on limit is load-bearing. A dollars-only field cannot express "20 PT visits/year" — the most common real-world cap. One discriminator covers both, and the adjudicator picks the column from it.

+
  • 12 rules exercise every branch: full_coverage, copay, coinsurance, visit-limit, dollar-limit, prior-auth, and excluded — plus any unlisted code falling through to NO_COVERAGE. Adding a 13th service is a data row, not a code change.

+

03 · The adjudication pipeline — gates, then math

Each line item walks one ordered pipeline. The first half is gates — any one fails, the line short-circuits to a clean denial. The second half is math — it always runs to completion and moves money.
+7bDollar-limit straddledollars + plan-pay exceeds remainingcap plan, shortfall→member, line stays APPROVEDMATH
+8OOP capmember share would pass oop_maxrefund excess to plan, OOP_MAX_REACHEDMATH

+
An MRI line, billed_cents: 120000, but prior_auth_present: false. Step 4 fails. The line stops immediately — steps 5–9 never run, no math, no deductible touched. Result: DENIED, PRIOR_AUTH_REQUIRED, payable 0, member owes 0, deltas all 0.

+
  • The pipeline has two halves with different jobs: gates 0–5 can only deny and stop; math 6–9 can only compute and continue. No step does both.

+
  • A failed gate is a clean decision (HTTP 200 + reason), not an error. It short-circuits before any money moves — so a denied line is cheap and fully explainable.

+
  • The straddle (7b) and OOP cap (8) are the only steps that adjust rather than deny: they cap a payable and re-attribute the shortfall, leaving the line APPROVED.

+

04 · The cost-share switch

Step 7 is one switch on cost_share.type — exactly one mechanism per rule, never stacked. The deductible is touched by coinsurance only; copay waives it and full_coverage ignores it.
+copaywaived; accrues to OOP onlymin(copay, allowed)allowed − member[APPROVED, COPAY_APPLIED]

+
The coinsurance formula — the only non-trivial branch
+plan = allowed − member // computed last → shares always sum

+
allowed = 20000c. Member 0c, plan 20000c. Deductible and OOP untouched by the mechanism. [APPROVED].

+
allowed = 18000c, copay 2500c. Member = min(2500, 18000) = 2500c; plan = 15500c. Deductible waived; 2500c accrues to OOP. [APPROVED, COPAY_APPLIED].

+
(c) coinsurance — 20% on a $1200 MRI, $300 deductible remaining

+
allowed = 120000c, remainingDeductible = 30000c. dedPortion = 30000c; remainder = 90000c; coinsPortion = round(0.20 × 90000) = 18000c; member = 48000c; plan = 120000 − 48000 = 72000c. [APPROVED, DEDUCTIBLE_APPLIED, COINSURANCE_APPLIED].

+
  • One rule = one mechanism. The switch is exhaustive over a discriminated union — incoherent "copay + coinsurance" rules can't be expressed, let alone mis-adjudicated.

+
  • plan = allowed − member is computed last, so member + plan always equal the allowed amount — no lost or duplicated cent, even with odd-cents rounding.

+
  • Only coinsurance reads/draws the deductible. Copay deliberately waives it and routes the flat charge to OOP — matching how real office-visit copays behave.

+

05 · A claim, end to end

The flagship worked example: one member, three line items, accumulators moving line by line. Watch the deductible fill and the money split — then the three lines roll up to one claim status.

+
DEDUCTIBLE met 20000c · OOP met 20000c · no limit rows

+
L1 · unknown code — no matching rule

+
copay = min(2500, 18000) = member 2500c; plan 15500c. Deductible waived; copay → OOP. [APPROVED, COPAY_APPLIED]

+
remainingDeductible = 50000 − 20000 = 30000c; dedPortion = 30000c; remainder = 90000c; coinsPortion = round(0.20 × 90000) = 18000c; member 48000c; plan 72000c. OOP check: 22500 + 48000 = 70500c < 300000 → no clamp. [APPROVED, DEDUCTIBLE_APPLIED, COINSURANCE_APPLIED]

+
  • Cross-line state is real: L3 sees the deductible L2 left alone, draws the full remaining 30000c, and pushes it to met. Lines are processed in order against a single working snapshot.

+
  • Every covered line satisfies the invariant payable + member == billed ($155+$25=$180; $720+$480=$1200). The denied line is 0/0.

+
  • A mix of DENIED + APPROVED rolls up to PARTIALLY_APPROVED — plan pays $875, member owes $505. Re-running the same claim on the same starting snapshot reproduces these exact numbers.

-
  • The pipeline is composable pure functions, not a single mega-function. Each step takes (state, line_item, policy) and returns either continuation or short-circuit.

-
  • A line item can carry multiple reason codes. A typical approved claim carries DEDUCTIBLE_APPLIED + COPAY_APPLIED + COINSURANCE_APPLIED simultaneously.

-
  • The writeAccumulator step happens exactly once, at the end. No partial writes, no race conditions within a single adjudication.

-
  • Adding a new reason code is localized: one new step (or one branch in an existing step), one explanation template, one test. The shape of the system absorbs the change.

+
  • The dimension discriminator avoids a column-per-service-code schema. One row shape handles deductible, OOP, and N per-service limits; a new limited service adds a lazily-created LIMIT: row, not a migration.

+
  • Cross-claim memory is the whole point. The before-state isn't theoretical — it's a prior claim's residue. Limits exhaust correctly because every claim reads then increments the same keyed totals.

+
  • One shape, two units. used_cents vs used_count keeps dollar caps and visit caps in the same table while preserving the unit's meaning — no float, no overloaded single column.

+

07 · Decisions that shaped the engine

Three resolved judgment calls behind the engine's behavior — each one trades a real alternative for determinism and explainability under a 48-hour, no-reviewer-queue scope.

+
A required-auth line with prior_auth_present: false becomes a clean DENIED carrying PRIOR_AUTH_REQUIRED, payable 0.

+
Why: there is no human-reviewer queue in scope. A NEEDS_REVIEW line would never resolve and would freeze claim aggregation at UNDER_REVIEW forever. A clean deny is deterministic and fully explainable.

+
Every denial returns 200 with status + reason + explanation. Only malformed/identity-failed input is 4xx.

+
Why: an HTTP error explains nothing to a member and would break "5 line items, 1 denied" and "explain why it was denied." A denied line is a processed line.

+
When a line omits prior_auth_present, treat it as true. The PRIOR_AUTH_REQUIRED path fires only on an explicit false.

+
Why: most services don't require prior auth, so a true default keeps the common input frictionless; callers assert false only when relevant. (Documented as a demo simplification — real systems treat absence as unauthorized.)

+
  • Every decision is grounded in a scope constraint (no reviewer queue, 48h budget), not preference — and each is reversible at a single seam if the constraint lifts.

+
  • The decision/error boundary is the engine's core contract: business denials are data (200 + reason), only bad input is a protocol error (4xx).

+
  • The true default makes the happy path frictionless while keeping the denial path one explicit false away — the divergence from real systems is named, not hidden.

+    and PHI is kept structurally unreachable by the engine. Together these make the system
+    auditable, consistent, and honest about sensitive data without over-building.

+
(a) Adjudications are append-only

+

Re-adjudication never mutates the original row. It reverses the line's original accumulator deltas, reapplies the new ones, and appends a new Adjudication. History is the audit trail.
+      <div style="background:#f8fafc; border-radius:8px; padding:16px; border:1px solid var(--border);">

+
(b) One transaction per line item

+

The decision write and every accumulator increment (deductible / OOP / limit) commit together in a single better-sqlite3 transaction. Synchronous single-writer → no interleaving, no half-applied decision.
+      <div style="background:#f8fafc; border-radius:8px; padding:16px; border:1px solid var(--border);">

+
(c) PHI is structurally unreachable

+

Member.name/dob and Claim.provider/diagnosis_code are sensitive. The engine input type keys on member_id only — it cannot read PHI because those fields aren't in its input shape. Column-level encryption-at-rest is documented as a candidate, not over-built for a 24–48h take-home.
+    <div style="margin-top:20px; padding:14px 16px; background:#f8fafc; border-radius:8px; border:1px dashed var(--border);">
+ Mini-example — disputing a denied MRI line
+      <svg viewBox="0 0 880 110" role="img" aria-label="Dispute appends a new adjudication" style="margin-top:10px;">
+          <marker id="ah" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="var(--muted)"/></marker>
+        <rect class="box" x="10" y="30" width="200" height="50" style="stroke:var(--red);"/>
+        <rect class="box" x="300" y="30" width="200" height="50" style="stroke:var(--muted); stroke-dasharray:4 3;"/>
+        <rect class="box" x="590" y="30" width="270" height="50" style="stroke:var(--teal);"/>

+

The original DENIED row is preserved verbatim; the new APPROVED row is appended with a DISPUTED_OVERRIDE reason, and the line's accumulator deltas are reversed then reapplied — both writes in one transaction.

-
  • Claim state is derived, not stored. claimStateFrom(items) is a pure function. This eliminates an entire class of "claim says approved but line items say denied" bugs.

-
  • The aggregation table has exhaustive test coverage: each row is one test case. NEEDS_REVIEW overrides everything else, which is the rule a real claims adjuster would expect.

-
  • The dispute loop reopens any terminal state. From a state-machine perspective, terminal isn't actually terminal — it's "terminal-until-disputed."

-
  • The PAID transition is the only one that requires a side effect (recording payment). Everything else is in-memory state arithmetic.

+
  • Append-only is the audit story. You can always answer "what did we decide, when, and why" — the original decision survives every dispute, so there's no destructive overwrite to reconstruct.

+
  • Atomicity is per line item. Decision + accumulator increments are one transaction, so the accumulator can never drift from the decisions that produced it — even mid-claim.

+
  • PHI minimization is enforced by the type system, not by discipline. The engine literally has no field to read diagnosis_code from; encryption-at-rest is named as the next step rather than half-implemented.

+

09 · State machines & aggregation

The engine produces the line-item state directly; the claim state is derived by aggregating its lines — never set directly. NEEDS_REVIEW is now reachable only through a dispute reopen.
+<defs><marker id="ah" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="7" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L7,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#64748b"/></marker></defs>
+<rect x="10" y="55" width="90" height="36" rx="8" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#64748b"/><text x="55" y="78" text-anchor="middle">PENDING</text>
+<rect x="200" y="10" width="100" height="36" rx="8" fill="#f0fdfa" stroke="#14b8a6"/><text x="250" y="33" text-anchor="middle" fill="#0f766e">APPROVED</text>
+<rect x="200" y="55" width="100" height="36" rx="8" fill="#fef2f2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="250" y="78" text-anchor="middle" fill="#b91c1c">DENIED</text>
+<rect x="200" y="100" width="120" height="36" rx="8" fill="#fffbeb" stroke="#f59e0b"/><text x="260" y="123" text-anchor="middle" fill="#b45309">NEEDS_REVIEW</text>
+<rect x="430" y="55" width="80" height="36" rx="8" fill="#eef2ff" stroke="#6366f1"/><text x="470" y="78" text-anchor="middle" fill="#4338ca">PAID</text>
+<path d="M470,91 C470,140 560,140 560,73 L560,55" fill="none" stroke="#6366f1" stroke-dasharray="4 3" marker-end="url(#ah)"/>
+mix of APPROVED + DENIED, or any dollar-straddle partialPARTIALLY_APPROVED
+Mini-example: the worked claim from §05 — lines [DENIED, APPROVED, APPROVED] → a mix → claim PARTIALLY_APPROVED. Flip L2/L3 to disputed and re-adjudicating → claim UNDER_REVIEW.

+
  • Claim state is derived, not stored: claimStatusFrom(lines) is a pure aggregation, killing the "claim says approved but lines say denied" class of bug.

+
  • PARTIALLY_APPROVED is claim-level only — never a line state. A straddled line is itself APPROVED with a LIMIT_EXCEEDED note.

+
  • NEEDS_REVIEW survives as a state but is now reached only via a dispute reopen — prior-auth-missing is a clean DENIED (decision #8), so it never freezes a fresh claim.

+

10 · The build order — 27 test-first cycles

The engine is test-driven one behavior per red→green cycle, in this order. Cycles 1–25 are pure (a plain object, no DB); only 26–27 touch SQLite. This is the coding checklist.

+
  • copay, allowed > copay → member = copay, plan = allowed − copay, deductible untouched

+
  • copay, allowed < copay → member = allowed, plan = 0 (min clamp)

+
  • coinsurance, deductible already met → member = round(rate·allowed), plan = rest

+
  • coinsurance, allowed < remaining deductible → all to deductible, coins 0, plan 0

+
  • coinsurance deductible crossing → split ded + coins; shares sum to allowed

+
  • coinsurance odd cents (20% of 3333) → deterministic rounding; member + plan == allowed

+
  • visits, used ≥ count → LIMIT_EXCEEDED, payable 0 (whole-visit, no straddle)

+
  • dollars, payable within remaining → approved, limit_inc = plan pay

+
  • dollars straddle → plan caps at remaining, shortfall→member, LIMIT_EXCEEDED, line APPROVED

+
  • member share would pass oop_max → excess refunded to plan, OOP_MAX_REACHED

+
  • accumulator already at oop_max → next line plan pays 100%, member 0

+
  • two coinsurance lines, one claim → line 2 sees line 1's advanced deductible

+
  • re-run identical claim on identical snapshot → identical results

+
  • full claim through the real store: accumulators persist, adjudications append-only, one txn

+
  • dispute reopens a line, re-adjudicates, preserves the original immutably (last)

+
  • The order is gates → cost-share → limits → OOP → cross-line → aggregation → persistence: each cycle adds exactly one behavior on top of green, so a failing test always names the behavior that broke.

+
  • Cycles 1–25 need no database — they run against a plain accumulator snapshot object. The pure core is fully proven before SQLite ever appears.

+
  • Determinism (cycle 21) and immutable disputes (cycle 27) are explicit tests, not hopes: re-running a claim on the same snapshot must reproduce identical cents.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
Claims System · Visual Reference

Four diagrams that explain the whole system: money flow, domain model, adjudication pipeline, and state machines. Use this for pairing-round walkthroughs and to align any future agent (or human) on the model.

01 · The money flow

A claim is a request for reimbursement. Adjudication splits the billed amount across member-owed slices (deductible, copay, coinsurance) and the insurer's payable share. The order matters: change it and every downstream dollar is wrong.

What this proves

  • Adjudication is deterministic. Same inputs → same outputs, every time. This is testable.
  • Money is stored as integer cents. Float arithmetic on money is the most common claims bug in industry.
  • The EOB is not a separate report — it falls out of the adjudication's reason codes. One source of truth.

02 · The domain model

Two bounded contexts. The adjudication context is pure functions, no I/O. The submission context handles the wire envelope. The seam between them is the only place persistence happens. This separation is what keeps the engine testable.

What this proves

  • CoverageRule is data, not code. A typed config object — not a DSL — is the 80/20 within 48h.
  • Accumulator is the system's short-term memory. Reading it before adjudication and writing it after is the only transactional step the engine has.
  • The Claim → LineItem 1:N relationship is why partial approvals exist. The system grades each line independently and rolls up.
  • Dispute doesn't mutate the original adjudication — it reopens a line item and produces a new one with a DISPUTED_OVERRIDE reason. History is preserved.

03 · The 11-step adjudication pipeline

Each line item runs through these 11 steps in this exact order. Steps 1-5 can short-circuit with a denial reason. Steps 6-11 do the arithmetic and always run to completion. Each step is a pure function. Each reason code has at least one test.

What this proves

  • The pipeline is composable pure functions, not a single mega-function. Each step takes (state, line_item, policy) and returns either continuation or short-circuit.
  • A line item can carry multiple reason codes. A typical approved claim carries DEDUCTIBLE_APPLIED + COPAY_APPLIED + COINSURANCE_APPLIED simultaneously.
  • The writeAccumulator step happens exactly once, at the end. No partial writes, no race conditions within a single adjudication.
  • Adding a new reason code is localized: one new step (or one branch in an existing step), one explanation template, one test. The shape of the system absorbs the change.

04 · State machines & aggregation

Two state machines move independently. The line-item machine is what the engine produces directly. The claim machine is derived from line-item states via the aggregation function — it is never set directly.

What this proves

  • Claim state is derived, not stored. claimStateFrom(items) is a pure function. This eliminates an entire class of "claim says approved but line items say denied" bugs.
  • The aggregation table has exhaustive test coverage: each row is one test case. NEEDS_REVIEW overrides everything else, which is the rule a real claims adjuster would expect.
  • The dispute loop reopens any terminal state. From a state-machine perspective, terminal isn't actually terminal — it's "terminal-until-disputed."
  • The PAID transition is the only one that requires a side effect (recording payment). Everything else is in-memory state arithmetic.
Generated for the realfast FDE Level-1 take-home · drop into docs/ as visual reference for the round-2 pairing session.

Planning is done — with time to spare before 6pm. Here's where everything landed.

## ✅ Complete
- **[docs/adjudication-plan.md](docs/adjudication-plan.md)** — the C3 reference (written): pipeline, cost-share math, determinism, accumulator writeback, 27-cycle TDD order, worked example.
- **[infographics/visual-reference.html](infographics/visual-reference.html)** — rebuilt with **10 example-induced sections** (preview is live →):

```
01 Domain model (entities + ER diagram)      06 Accumulator (before/after memory)
02 Coverage rules as data (+12-rule seed)    07 Decisions (prior-auth, error/decision, default)
03 Pipeline — gates vs math                  08 Immutability · disputes · PHI
04 Cost-share switch (3 worked examples)     09 State machines & aggregation
05 A claim, end to end ($875 / $505)         10 Build order — 27 TDD cycles
```
Every concept is followed by real integer-cents numbers; the flagship 3-line claim threads through §04→05→06→09 so a reviewer can follow one example across the whole engine.

- **Trackers** (PRD, decisions, domain-model, TRACK) — already reconciled last turn and verified consistent.

## 🗺️ Status
```
[done]  Framing · design · adjudication plan · trackers · visual reference ✅
[now]   ▶ START TDD — cycle 1: "no rule → NO_COVERAGE"   (your 1-hour window)
[then]  cycles 2–25 pure engine · 26–27 SQLite
[hskpg] save this session's JSONL → ai-artifacts/03-design/
```

Two notes: the HTML's `<object>` SVG embeds were dropped — 3 of the 4 standalone SVGs were stale vs the cost-share-union model; the new inline visuals replace them and the `.svg` files stay on disk if you want to regenerate them later. And the assignment still needs **this session's JSONL in `ai-artifacts/03-design/`** before submission.

**Planning's locked. Want me to start TDD cycle 1 now** — load `tdd-discipline` + `insurance-domain` and write the first failing test for *"a line item with no coverage rule → DENIED, payable 0, NO_COVERAGE"*? That fits in your remaining window.

content.text:
Quick scope verdict before I bring in the architect: this is **in scope and worth strengthening** — the brief explicitly names *"the state machine of a claim vs. a line item"* and *"track the claim through its lifecycle,"* and a member-facing status timeline is exactly that. The risk isn't scope, it's **over-building** (event-sourcing the whole system). So the job is the *minimal* version.

Bringing in your **System & Solution Architect** to design the leanest possible approach, grounded in what's already locked:

input.prompt:
You are the SYSTEM & SOLUTION ARCHITECT for a 24-48h insurance claims take-home (TypeScript + SQLite/better-sqlite3 + vitest, test-first). Design the LEANEST possible status-transition audit log so a member gets a clear, ordered status timeline for a claim and its line items.

READ FIRST (reconcile with what's locked — do not contradict):
- /Users/raunaksingh/Documents/realfast/insurance/docs/domain-model.md   (entities, the two state machines, derived claim status)
- /Users/raunaksingh/Documents/realfast/insurance/docs/adjudication-plan.md (pipeline, append-only Adjudication, accumulator writeback, the 27-cycle TDD order, determinism)
- /Users/raunaksingh/Documents/realfast/insurance/docs/decisions.md       (append-only discipline, decisions #8/#12, dispute model)
- /Users/raunaksingh/Documents/realfast/insurance/PRD.md                   (4 endpoints, in/out of scope)

WHAT THE USER WANTS: a transition log capturing, per status change, { from, to, actor, reason, timestamp } for BOTH claims and line items — a current-status snapshot + history so the member can be told clearly where the claim is.

YOUR CONSTRAINTS — minimal code coverage, reuse over new machinery:
- The system already has append-only Adjudication records and a derived claim status. Do NOT duplicate the adjudication decision data; the transition log is the STATUS FLOW, not the money math.
- Do NOT event-source the system. Status columns stay the source of truth for "current"; the log is an append-only audit/timeline written at the SAME point a status changes.
- There is no auth (out of scope) — "actor" is a small enum/string (e.g. SYSTEM, MEMBER), not a user entity.

DELIVER, TIGHTLY (skimmable, this goes to a confused, time-boxed user):
1. **Scope call** — confirm in-scope and name the one over-build risk to avoid (2-3 lines).
2. **The design** — one shared polymorphic table vs two; give the exact row shape (columns + types, integer/text). Recommend one, briefly say why.
3. **The single chokepoint** — one `recordTransition(...)` helper called from exactly the places status changes (submit, each line adjudication result, aggregation roll-up, dispute reopen, settle/PAID). List those call sites. Show how this keeps the new code tiny and impossible to bypass.
4. **Determinism/immutability fit** — how it stays append-only and how timestamps avoid breaking the "re-run yields identical results" property (hint: timestamp is metadata, not part of the decision; or injected clock).
5. **Member-facing read** — how it surfaces (extend an existing endpoint vs a new one) for an ordered timeline.
6. **TDD impact** — how many NEW test cycles this adds and what they assert, slotted relative to the existing 27-cycle order.

content.text:
Docs read and reconciled. Everything below fits the locked model with zero contradictions. Here's the lean design.

## 1. Scope call

**In scope:** an append-only `status_transition` audit log capturing `{ entity, from, to, actor, reason, timestamp }` for both claims and line items, written at the exact moment a status column changes. Read back as an ordered timeline on the existing `GET /claims/:id`.

**The one over-build risk:** turning this into an event store. The status columns stay the source of truth for "current"; the log is a *parallel audit trail*, never replayed to derive state. If you ever find yourself reading the log to compute current status, you've drifted into event sourcing — stop.

## 2. The design — one shared polymorphic table

Recommend **one table**, not two. The row shape is identical for claims and lines; two tables would double the writer, the reader, and the tests for zero benefit. Discriminate with an `entity_type` column.

```sql
CREATE TABLE status_transition (
  id            INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,  -- also the stable tiebreak for ordering
  entity_type   TEXT    NOT NULL,                   -- 'CLAIM' | 'LINE_ITEM'
  entity_id     TEXT    NOT NULL,                   -- claim_id or line_item_id
  from_status   TEXT,                               -- NULL on the first/create transition
  to_status     TEXT    NOT NULL,
  actor         TEXT    NOT NULL,                   -- 'SYSTEM' | 'MEMBER'
  reason        TEXT    NOT NULL,                   -- short enum-ish tag: 'SUBMIT','ADJUDICATED','AGGREGATED','DISPUTE_REOPEN','SETTLED'
  seq           INTEGER NOT NULL,                   -- injected logical clock (see §4), monotonic per claim
  created_at    TEXT                                -- ISO wall-clock, METADATA ONLY, never read by logic/tests
);
CREATE INDEX idx_transition_entity ON status_transition(entity_type, entity_id, seq);
```

Why these choices:
- `from_status` nullable — the creation transition (`null → SUBMITTED`/`PENDING`) is real and worth logging; nullable beats a fake `NONE` sentinel.
- `reason` is a coarse *transition cause* tag, deliberately **not** a `ReasonCode`. The adjudication `reasons[]` already carries the money-math why on the Adjudication row — do not duplicate it here. This log answers "what moved and who moved it," not "how much."
- `seq` is the deterministic order key; `created_at` is human-facing metadata only.

## 3. The single chokepoint

One helper, called nowhere else but the status-mutation sites:

```ts
function recordTransition(
  db, tx,
  { entityType, entityId, from, to, actor, reason, seq }
): void   // INSERT one row. No reads, no derivation.
```

Make it **impossible to bypass** by pairing it with the write: the only function allowed to UPDATE a `status` column also calls `recordTransition` in the *same* transaction. Wrap them:

```ts
function setStatus(db, tx, entityType, entityId, to, actor, reason, seq) {
  // UPDATE <table> SET status = to WHERE id = entityId
  // recordTransition(...)  ← same tx
}
```

If every status write goes through `setStatus`, the log can never drift from the columns. New code is ~2 small functions.

**Call sites (exactly these five):**

| # | Where | entity | from → to | actor | reason |
|---|-------|--------|-----------|-------|--------|
| 1 | Intake persist (C2) | CLAIM + each LINE | null → SUBMITTED / null → PENDING | SYSTEM | SUBMIT |
| 2 | Each line adjudication result (C3 writeback) | LINE_ITEM | PENDING → APPROVED\|DENIED | SYSTEM | ADJUDICATED |
| 3 | Aggregation roll-up | CLAIM | SUBMITTED → APPROVED\|PARTIALLY_APPROVED\|DENIED\|UNDER_REVIEW | SYSTEM | AGGREGATED |
| 4 | Dispute reopen (C6/cycle 27) | LINE_ITEM (+ CLAIM → UNDER_REVIEW) | DENIED → NEEDS_REVIEW | MEMBER | DISPUTE_REOPEN |
| 5 | Settle / pay | LINE_ITEM → PAID, CLAIM → PAID | SYSTEM | SETTLED |

Note: dispute also produces a *follow-on* re-adjudication transition (NEEDS_REVIEW → APPROVED\|DENIED via call site 2's path), which is exactly the auto-re-adjudicate behavior in decision #8 / assumption #7 — no new site needed.

## 4. Determinism / immutability fit

- **Append-only:** the table is INSERT-only. Never UPDATE or DELETE a transition row. This mirrors the Adjudication append-only discipline exactly — a dispute reverses deltas and appends; it never mutates history.
- **Timestamp is metadata, not a decision input.** `created_at` is wall-clock and is *never* read by the adjudicator, the aggregator, or any test assertion. The "re-run yields identical results" property is about `status / payable / reasons / deltas` — none of which this log feeds.
- **Ordering uses `seq`, an injected logical clock, not the clock.** Pass a monotonic counter (per-claim, starting at 0, incremented per transition) through the same caller that owns the snapshot-then-writeback transaction. Re-running a claim produces identical `seq` values and identical row content except `created_at`. So timeline *order* is deterministic; only the human-readable timestamp varies — which is correct, because a timestamp that didn't vary on a real re-run would be a lie.
- Everything writes inside the **one SQLite transaction per claim/line** already described in the adjudication plan — better-sqlite3's synchronous single-writer guarantees no interleaving, so `seq` and row order stay consistent.

## 5. Member-facing read

**Extend `GET /claims/:id`** — do not add a new endpoint (PRD locks 4 routes; a 5th is scope creep).

Add a `timeline` field to the existing claim response: the current status snapshot is already the `status` columns; the history is a `SELECT … WHERE entity_id IN (claim, its lines) ORDER BY seq, id`.

```jsonc
{
  "id": "clm_1", "status": "PARTIALLY_APPROVED",
  "line_items": [ /* each with its own current status */ ],
  "timeline": [
    { "entity": "CLAIM",     "id": "clm_1", "from": null,       "to": "SUBMITTED",          "actor": "SYSTEM", "reason": "SUBMIT",      "at": "..." },
    { "entity": "LINE_ITEM", "id": "li_1",  "from": "PENDING",  "to": "APPROVED",           "actor": "SYSTEM", "reason": "ADJUDICATED", "at": "..." },
    { "entity": "LINE_ITEM", "id": "li_2",  "from": "PENDING",  "to": "DENIED",             "actor": "SYSTEM", "reason": "ADJUDICATED", "at": "..." },
    { "entity": "CLAIM",     "id": "clm_1", "from": "SUBMITTED","to": "PARTIALLY_APPROVED",  "actor": "SYSTEM", "reason": "AGGREGATED",  "at": "..." }
  ]
}
```

Ordered globally by `seq` gives the member one readable, deterministic story. `GET /:id/explanation` stays the money-EOB; this stays the status-flow.

## 6. TDD impact — 4 new cycles

Tests 1–25 are pure and untouched (this log needs a store, so it lives with 26–27). Slot **4 new cycles after 27**, renumbered 28–31:

| Cycle | Asserts |
|-------|---------|
| 28 | `recordTransition` appends one row; fields `{entity_type, entity_id, from, to, actor, reason, seq}` match; `from` is null on create. |
| 29 | Full claim submit→adjudicate→aggregate logs the expected ordered transition set (SUBMIT lines + claim, ADJUDICATED per line, AGGREGATED claim) in correct `seq` order. |
| 30 | Re-running the same claim yields **identical** transition rows except `created_at` — locks determinism of `seq` + content. |
| 31 | Dispute reopen logs `DENIED → NEEDS_REVIEW` (actor MEMBER) **then** the auto re-adjudication transition; original rows untouched (append-only). |

Cycle 31 naturally extends the existing cycle-27 dispute test — they share a setup, keeping it cheap.

## 7. Explicitly DO NOT build

- **Full event sourcing / replay** — status columns remain the source of truth; the log is never read to compute current state.
- **A second table for line items** — one polymorphic table only.
- **A separate reviewer actor** — actor is `SYSTEM | MEMBER` only; there's no human-review queue (decision #8, assumption #7).
- **A 5th REST endpoint** — extend `GET /claims/:id`, don't add `/timeline`.
- **Reason-code duplication** — `reason` is a coarse cause tag, not the adjudication `ReasonCode`; money-why stays on the Adjudication row.
- **A `user` / actor entity, auth, or attribution beyond the enum** — out of scope per PRD.
- **Mutating/soft-deleting transition rows** — append-only, no corrections-in-place.
- **An `illegal transition` enforcement engine here** — guard validation belongs in the state machine (C4); this log only records what *did* happen.

## Docs this would touch (additions, no contradictions)

- `docs/domain-model.md` — add a `StatusTransition` row to the entity table and note it under both state machines as the audit trail. The "Still open: transition tables" line is the natural home.
- `docs/adjudication-plan.md` — add cycles 28–31 to the TDD build order; note `recordTransition` rides inside the existing per-claim transaction.
- `docs/decisions.md` — one new entry: "#14 — status audit log: shared polymorphic table, injected `seq` clock, not event-sourced."

No locked decision is contradicted: append-only discipline (decisions §, adjudication-plan §4) is reinforced, the 4-endpoint lock (PRD) is preserved, determinism (adjudication-plan §Determinism) is preserved by treating the timestamp as metadata.

One flag for you: this assumes a **PAID / SETTLED** transition path exists in v1. The state machines include `→ PAID` but I don't see a settle endpoint in the 4 routes — if "PAID" is never actually triggered in v1, drop call site #5 and cycle coverage for SETTLED, leaving 3 call sites and the timeline ending at aggregation. Want me to confirm whether PAID is reachable before we lock the call-site list?

Relevant files: `/Users/raunaksingh/Documents/realfast/insurance/docs/domain-model.md`, `/Users/raunaksingh/Documents/realfast/insurance/docs/adjudication-plan.md`, `/Users/raunaksingh/Documents/realfast/insurance/docs/decisions.md`, `/Users/raunaksingh/Documents/realfast/insurance/PRD.md`.

content.text:
Architect's back. Verdict and the lean plan:

## ✅ In scope — and minimal
Strengthening the lifecycle directly serves *"state management"* (a named rubric signal). The only trap is **event-sourcing the system** — we won't. Status columns stay the source of truth; this is a **parallel audit log**, never replayed.

## The design — one table, one chokepoint
**One append-only table** for both claims and line items (no second table):

```
status_transition(
  entity_type 'CLAIM'|'LINE_ITEM', entity_id,
  from_status, to_status, actor 'SYSTEM'|'MEMBER',
  reason 'SUBMIT'|'ADJUDICATED'|'AGGREGATED'|'DISPUTE_REOPEN'|'SETTLED',
  seq,            ← logical clock, deterministic ordering
  created_at )    ← wall-clock, metadata only
```

**One helper makes it impossible to bypass:** the *only* function that writes a `status` column also logs the transition, in the same transaction:
```
setStatus(entity, to, actor, reason) → UPDATE status + recordTransition()
```
That's ~2 small functions. Logged at exactly **5 points**: submit · each line adjudicated · claim roll-up · dispute reopen · settle(PAID).

## Why it stays clean
- **`reason` is a coarse cause tag, NOT the adjudication `ReasonCode`** — the money-why already lives on the immutable Adjudication row. No duplication.
- **Determinism preserved** — ordering uses the injected `seq`; the timestamp is metadata no test or math reads. Re-runs produce identical rows except `created_at`.
- **No 5th endpoint** — surfaces as a `timeline` field on the existing `GET /claims/:id`. The member gets one ordered story.
- **+4 TDD cycles** (28–31): records a row · full-claim ordered timeline · re-run determinism · dispute appends (original untouched).

## One thing to confirm before I lock it 👇
The architect flagged it: **is `PAID`/settle actually reachable in v1?** Your PRD has 4 endpoints with no settle route, but the state machines include `→ PAID`.
- **If PAID is real** → keep 5 call sites (need a settle trigger).
- **If not** → drop the SETTLED site; timeline ends at aggregation (3 sites, even leaner).

Which is it? Then I'll fold this into `domain-model.md` + `adjudication-plan.md` (cycles 28–31) + one decision entry — a ~10-min doc update, and you start TDD.

20	**Reversible?:** Low cost early, high cost later.
21	
22	### 2 — Persistence: SQLite (better-sqlite3) over Postgres
23	
24	**Chose:** SQLite via better-sqlite3.
25	**Over:** Postgres / Docker-compose stack.
26	**Trade-off:** A reviewer clones and runs with zero DB setup, and the synchronous driver
27	keeps adjudication deterministic and easy to test. Cost: no real concurrency story; fine
28	for a demo, called out as a limitation in self-review.
29	**Reversible?:** Moderate — schema is portable; the synchronous-driver assumption is not.
30	
31	### 3 — Rule representation: typed config over a DSL / rules engine
32	
33	**Chose:** Coverage rules as typed config records, applied by a fixed-order adjudicator.
34	**Over:** A rules DSL or a pluggable rules engine.
35	**Trade-off:** Typed config is reviewable, type-checked, and enough for the rules in scope.
36	A DSL is meta-machinery the assignment explicitly does not reward and would eat the 48h
37	budget. Cost: adding a fundamentally new *kind* of rule means code, not config.
38	**Reversible?:** Yes — the adjudicator is the seam; a DSL could be layered later.
39	
40	### 4 — Money: integer cents everywhere
41	
42	**Chose:** All monetary values as integer cents; percentages applied with explicit rounding
43	at the cents step.
44	**Over:** Floating-point dollars, or a decimal library.
45	**Trade-off:** Integers make money math exact and tests trustworthy — float math on money
46	(coinsurance %, accumulation) is the most common claims bug. Cost: must round consciously
47	where coinsurance produces fractional cents (member + plan shares must sum to allowed).
48	**Reversible?:** Hard — touches every amount; chosen deliberately up front.
49	
50	### 5 — Interface: REST API (fastify) over CLI or web UI
51	
52	**Chose:** Four REST endpoints via fastify.
53	**Over:** CLI, or a web UI.
54	**Trade-off:** REST is the clearest way to demonstrate the domain to a reviewer and maps
55	1:1 to the operations (submit, fetch, explain, dispute). A UI would burn budget on
56	non-rubric surface. _Confirm this stands after framing._
57	**Reversible?:** Yes — the domain core is interface-agnostic.
58	
59	### 6 — Cost-share: a discriminated union, not nullable copay + coinsurance fields
60	
61	**Chose:** `costShare: { type: "full_coverage" } | { type: "copay"; copayCents } | { type: "coinsurance"; rate }` — one mechanism per rule.
62	**Over:** a flat record with both `copayCents` and `coinsuranceRate` applied in sequence.
63	**Trade-off:** Research across UHC, Aetna, Cigna, BCBS, and ACA showed real benefits use
64	*one* mechanism — preventive is full coverage, office/ER/urgent are copay, imaging/surgery/
65	hospital are coinsurance after deductible. A union makes the model *say* that, turns the
66	adjudicator into an exhaustive switch, and rejects incoherent rules at the type level. Cost:
67	the rare copay-then-coinsurance case (ER) is approximated by its dominant component.
68	**Reversible?:** Yes — a 4th union variant can be added; the adjudicator switch is the seam.
69	
70	### 7 — Annual limit: unit-typed (`none` | `dollars` | `visits`), not dollars-only
71	
72	**Chose:** `limit: { unit: "none" } | { unit: "dollars"; amountCents } | { unit: "visits"; count }`,
73	with the accumulator's `limit_used` tracked in the same unit.
74	**Over:** a single `annualLimitCents` field.
75	**Trade-off:** The brief's one concrete example ("$Y per year") is a dollar limit — but the
76	*most common* real limit is a visit/day cap (PT 20 visits/yr, SNF 60 days/yr), which a
77	cents-only field literally cannot express. The unit discriminator handles both on one code
78	path. Cost: the accumulator and limit check become unit-aware (small). This is a deliberate
79	step beyond the brief's literal ask, justified by domain research.
80	**Reversible?:** Moderate — touches the accumulator's limit column and the limit step.
81	
82	### 8 — Prior-auth missing → clean DENY (RESOLVED in C3 brainstorm)
83	
84	> **Status: RESOLVED** (C3 brainstorm, this session). Supersedes the earlier provisional
85	> `NEEDS_REVIEW` lean.
86	
87	**Chose:** A prior-auth-required line with no auth present → a clean `DENIED` line carrying
88	`PRIOR_AUTH_REQUIRED`, payable 0, with an explanation. It is an adjudication **decision**
89	(HTTP 200), never an error.
90	**Over:** Routing it to `NEEDS_REVIEW` (the brief's *"1 needs review"* lean).
91	**Trade-off:** With no human-reviewer queue in scope, a `NEEDS_REVIEW` line would never
92	resolve and would freeze claim aggregation at `UNDER_REVIEW` forever. A clean deny is
93	deterministic and fully explainable — the brief's "explain why it was denied" is satisfied by
94	the reason code + sentence. Cost: we don't showcase a review-queue flow (out of scope anyway).
95	`NEEDS_REVIEW` survives as a state but is reached **only** via a dispute reopen, and clears by
96	immediate auto re-adjudication (Q4 lean). This resolves the old Q6.
97	**Reversible?:** Yes — prior-auth returns a single routing flag; swapping to `NEEDS_REVIEW`
98	is a one-line change if a queue is ever added.
99	
100	### 9 — Capture `diagnosis_code` + `provider` as encrypted, non-adjudicated PHI
101	
102	**Chose:** Capture `diagnosis_code` and `provider` on the Claim, store them as sensitive
103	(encryption-at-rest candidate), and **never** read them in adjudication.
104	**Over:** Omitting them entirely (the earlier research lean).
105	**Trade-off:** The brief explicitly names them as sensitive health data; capturing them is
106	where we *demonstrate* the PHI-handling the brief is testing. Cost: PHI we must protect for
107	zero math benefit — accepted deliberately, documented as the showcase of the PHI stance.
108	**Reversible?:** Yes — they're inert fields.
109	
110	### 10 — Claim + LineItem shape, closed 12-code catalog, `reasons[]` array
111	
112	**Chose:** Claim = `{ id, member_id, service_date (claim-level), provider, diagnosis_code,
113	status (derived) }`; LineItem = `{ id, claim_id, service_code, billed_cents, units,
114	prior_auth_present, status, fingerprint }`; a **closed 12-entry `service_code` catalog**;
115	Adjudication carries a `reasons[]` array.
116	**Over:** Per-line service dates; an open service vocabulary; a single dominant reason code.
117	**Trade-off:** Claim-level date + closed catalog keep v1 simple and make `NO_COVERAGE`
118	well-defined. `reasons[]` gives a richer EOB breakdown than one dominant code. Cost: can't
119	model a claim spanning multiple service dates (documented).
120	**Reversible?:** Moderate for the date; easy for the rest.
121	
122	### 11 — C2 intake: reject = HTTP 4xx, never persisted (no `REJECTED` state)
123	
124	**Chose:** Structural/identity failures (bad shape, non-integer cents, future/invalid date,
125	**unknown member**) → HTTP `4xx` with `{ errors: [{ field, code, message }] }`, nothing
126	persisted. Member *existence* is an intake reject; policy *active-on-date* is an
127	adjudication `POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE` deny.
128	**Over:** Persisting a `REJECTED` claim row for auditability.
129	**Trade-off:** Matches the industry reject≠deny boundary (a reject never enters the system,
130	no appeal) and avoids bloating the frozen claim state machine. Cost: no built-in audit trail
131	of bad submissions — if needed, log them separately, not as Claim rows.
132	**Reversible?:** Yes — a reject log can be added without touching the claim machine.
133	
134	### 12 — Adjudication outcomes are decisions (HTTP 200), not errors
135	
136	**Chose:** Every adjudication outcome — including all denials (`NO_COVERAGE`, `EXCLUDED`,
137	`PRIOR_AUTH_REQUIRED`, `LIMIT_EXCEEDED`, `POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE`, `DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM`) — returns
138	HTTP `200` with `status` + `reason` + `explanation`. A denied line is a *processed* line.
139	Only malformed/identity-failed **input** is an HTTP `4xx` (the C2 intake reject, decision #11).
140	**Over:** Treating a business denial (e.g. prior-auth missing) as an HTTP error.
141	**Trade-off:** Errors explain nothing to a member and would break the brief's *"5 line items,
142	1 denied"* scenario and *"explain why it was denied."* A decision carries the explanation.
143	Cost: callers must read per-line status, not just the HTTP code — correct and intended.
144	**Reversible?:** N/A — this is the core contract of the engine.
145	
146	### 13 — `prior_auth_present` defaults to `true` on input (absence = auth present)
147	
148	**Chose:** When a line item omits `prior_auth_present`, treat it as `true`. The
149	`PRIOR_AUTH_REQUIRED` denial path fires only on an explicit `false`.
150	**Over:** Defaulting to `false` (absence = no auth).
151	**Trade-off:** Most services don't require prior auth, so a `true` default keeps the common
152	input frictionless; callers assert `false` only when relevant. Cost: a caller who forgets to
153	send `false` for an auth-required service is assumed to have had auth — an accepted demo
154	simplification, documented here. (Real systems treat absence as unauthorized.)
155	**Reversible?:** Yes — a one-line default change.
156	
157	---
158	
159	## Decisions resolved this framing session
160	
161	- **Prior-auth modeling** (Q1) — boolean precondition; missing → `PRIOR_AUTH_REQUIRED`, payable 0. PPO reduce-to-50% penalty documented as a divergence, not built.
162	- **Out-of-network** (Q2) — in-network only for v1 (allowed == billed); OON/unlisted service → `NO_COVERAGE`. Network/metal/family fields omitted.
163	- **Accumulator period** (Q3) — fixed plan-year window keyed on the policy.
164	
165	## Decisions still open (resolve and move up)
166	
167	- **Dispute resolution (Q4)** — auto re-adjudicate vs. reviewer queue (leaning auto, immutable
168	  original). C6 — does not block core adjudication TDD (cycles 1–25); confirm before cycle 27.
169	
170	## Decisions resolved (moved up)
171	
172	- **Duplicate handling (Q5)** — RESOLVED: fingerprint computed at intake; soft `DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM` decided at adjudication. See decision #11 / TRACK #12.
173	- **C3 adjudication behavior** — RESOLVED (this session): full pipeline, cost-share math,
174	  determinism, accumulator writeback, and the TDD build order are planned in
175	  [`docs/adjudication-plan.md`](adjudication-plan.md). Ready to test-drive.
176	- **Prior-auth routing (was Q6 / decision #8)** — RESOLVED: clean DENY, not `NEEDS_REVIEW`.
177	  See decision #8.
178	
179	## Assumptions about the domain
180	
181	1. Single currency (USD), integer cents.
182	2. One policy per member per plan year; no coordination of benefits.
183	3. Allowed amount == billed amount (no fee schedule lookup).
184	4. Plan year is a fixed window on the policy; accumulators align to it.
185	5. Prior auth is a recorded precondition, not a workflow.
186	6. Determinism over wall-clock; concurrency beyond SQLite's single-writer is out of scope.
187	7. One cost-share mechanism per service; copay-then-coinsurance approximated by dominant component.
188	8. Limits are unit-typed (`dollars` or `visits`); supply-window/replacement-frequency limits out of scope.
189	9. `prior_auth_present` defaults to `true` on input (absence = auth present); denial fires only on explicit `false`.
190	10. Adjudication denials are decisions (HTTP 200 + reason + explanation); only malformed/identity input is HTTP 4xx.
191	
192	_Add to this list whenever the build forces a judgment call the assignment left open._
193
